--- SOURCE 2: Global Reporting Initiative: Literature review and research directions ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774

SUMMARY:
By analyzing 90 high-quality peer-reviewed articles, this systematic literature review aims to explore the state of research on the Global Reporting Initiative, which is the most widely adopted corporate sustainability reporting tool, and to provide critical thinking on the issues related to the reporting practice. Although other instruments are also gaining attention, i.e. the Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP), the Integrated Reporting (IR) framework and the Sustainability Accounting and Standard Board (SASB), GRI is still the most diffused and comprehensive (Goswami et al., 2023). The topic of non-financial or sustainability reporting has been extensively explored in previous literature reviews (e.g., Osobajo et al., 2022; Turzo et al., 2022; Hahn and Kühnen, 2013).

Global Reporting Initiative: Literature review and research directions
==============================================================================

Highlights
----------

*   •First systematic literature review on the Global Reporting Initiative. 
*   •Investigation of antecedent-process-outcome clusters. 
*   •A disconnect between micro and macro interests may lead to reports' quality issues. 
*   •Interconnections for future research are identified in a micro-meta-macro framework. 

Abstract
--------

Sustainability reporting will soon become a common practice in organizations of all sizes and sectors, driven by escalating pressures and evolving stakeholders’ expectations. By analyzing 90 high-quality peer-reviewed articles, this systematic literature review aims to explore the state of research on the Global Reporting Initiative, which is the most widely adopted corporate sustainability reporting tool, and to provide critical thinking on the issues related to the reporting practice. Results are classified in an antecedent-process-outcome framework and interactions between the clusters are provided in a micro-meta-macro setting to develop discussions and insights for future research. The findings suggest that the overall research on the Global Reporting Initiative is well-developed. However, the misalignment of private and public short and long-term interests that emerged from the analysis may be a reason for the existence of unintended disclosure behaviors and quality issues in reporting, such as greenwashing. Accordingly, future research directions are developed to bridge the identified gap.

1. Introduction
---------------

Interest in sustainability reporting has skyrocketed in recent years, driven by several factors, including increased awareness of sustainability issues, stakeholder scrutiny of corporate behavior and legal obligations ([Turzo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib138)). As a consequence, since the end of the 1990s, there has been an increasing trend in the number of firms that report their sustainability practices and performance. Based on the 2021 Fortune 500 ranking, 96% of the World's 250 largest companies by revenue publish a sustainability report and most of them adopt the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) as a reporting tool (KMPG, 2022).

The GRI Institution was founded in 1997 by Bob Massie and Allen White, who understood that a holistic standardized tool to align companies' and stakeholders' needs for transparent information about their sustainability impacts was missing ([Levy et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib75)). Over the years, numerous tools have been developed to assess and convey corporate sustainability. Nevertheless, GRI has emerged as the globally predominant and most comprehensive framework for sustainability reporting ([Afolabi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib1); [van Oorschot et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib140)). Although other instruments are also gaining attention, i.e. the Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP), the Integrated Reporting (IR) framework and the Sustainability Accounting and Standard Board (SASB), GRI is still the most diffused and comprehensive ([Goswami et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib47)). While CDP mostly focuses on environmental reporting, IR and SASB have a relatively recent inception, in 2009 and 2011, respectively. Furthermore, SASB is a sector-specific standard, predominantly adopted in the US ([Schooley and English, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib121); KMPG, 2022) and primarily driven by financial purposes ([Pizzi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib111)). On the other hand, IR integrates sustainability and financial aspects for conveying the economic value of sustainability initiatives implementation, therefore its scope is wider and possibly not suitable for the focus of this study, i.e. sustainability reporting. Additionally, IR has also faced criticism for its perceived failure to distinguish itself from purely financial reports ([de Villiers and Sharma, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib23)). In this context, GRI plays a crucial role in harmonizing sustainability reporting practices, thereby enhancing comparability and overall reliability ([Tschopp and Nastanski, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib135)).

As a consequence, GRI's sustainability focus and its wide diffusion make it an important tool for both scholars and managers. The reports are indeed a valuable data source on companies' sustainability management practices (e.g. [Massari and Giannoccaro, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib86); [van Oorschot et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib140)). Therefore, exploring how existing research treats and evaluates GRI information allows for better development of empirical studies and enhanced corporate sustainability management and evaluation. Also, studying GRI can improve the credibility and reliability of reports and limit greenwashing concerns.

The topic of non-financial or sustainability reporting has been extensively explored in previous literature reviews (e.g., [Osobajo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib102); [Turzo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib138); [Hahn and Kühnen, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib52)). While a universal standard for sustainability reporting is missing, a broader perspective on the topic proves beneficial. However, because of the abovementioned reasons, it is equally crucial to facilitate more targeted investigations. Notably, considering the widespread adoption of the GRI as an international standard and the active role played by the GRI in the development of the European Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), a thorough examination of this instrument is required. Specifically, GRI has been working along with the European Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG) to co-construct the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) in anticipation of the entry into force of CSRD regulation ([EFRAG-GRI, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib30)).

In general, there are only a few reviews on GRI, which predominantly fall into two categories: bibliometric or narrative. Regarding bibliometric reviews, some works have recently emerged (e.g. [E-Vahdati & Aripin, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib34); [Mougenot and Doussoulin, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib93); [Kulevicz et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib71)). However, most are limited in geographical ([Mougenot and Doussoulin, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib93)) or temporal scope ([Kulevicz et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib71); [Petera and Wagner, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib109)). While these types of analyses are crucial for identifying literature trends and interconnections, systematic literature reviews facilitate deeper analysis and interpretation, which is the most challenging task ([Zupic and Čater, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib154), p. 458). Moreover, from the comparison of results, a better understanding of the field can be discerned.

Narrative reviews on GRI often have a limited scope as well. For instance, some studies concentrated on particular industries, such as mining ([Fonseca et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib40)), while others predominantly explored quality issues and criticisms ([Zsóka and Vajkai, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib153)). Moreover, a review focused on the current state of knowledge regarding materiality, comparability, and assurance ([Luque-Vílchez et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib80)). We can therefore conclude that a comprehensive systematic literature review on GRI is still missing.

Given the established importance of GRI for societal actors, the increasing attention from policymakers on sustainability reporting and the lack of specific systematic reviews on the topic, this work aims to analyze the literature to contribute to improving future sustainability reporting practices for both scholars and practitioners. Specifically, the following research question and sub-questions are defined.
*   RQ: What can we learn from existing research on sustainability reporting and specifically on GRI? 
*   -RQ1: What are the key factors that characterize and distinguish GRI reporting from general sustainability reporting? 
*   -RQ2: What are the main merits and criticisms of GRI? 

To answer these questions, the existing literature was initially organized into an antecedent-process-outcome framework and further clustered into micro, meta, and macro perspectives. This approach offers a comprehensive overview of the GRI phenomenon and provides directions to guide future research. A main takeaway from this analysis is that GRI's success may depend on its ability to align organizations' goals and external interests in a win-win setting. This can help overcome some unintended biases and quality issues (i.e. greenwashing) that may derive from a misalignment of these objectives. To do so, GRI should be able to convey the message that economic, social, and environmental goals, although seemingly disconnected, can be achieved simultaneously.

This study contributes to both theory and management practice. In terms of academic contribution, this work represents the first systematic literature review focusing on the GRI, the dominant sustainability reporting tool to date. Furthermore, the critical evaluation of the literature allowed us to create interconnections between the different clusters and topics discussed. In this way, reflections on the causes of reports' quality issues and suggestions have been formulated to contribute to the current debate. Moreover, a better and more comprehensive picture of far-sighted future research has been highlighted. The future research directions are built leveraging prominent purchasing, supply chain and management research theories (e.g. paradox theory and complex adaptive systems) and methodologies (e.g. system dynamics modeling and action research). Regarding the managerial contribution, useful features of GRI implementation, such as barriers, determinants and outcomes, emerge from the analyzed studies, which may help practitioners understand implementation strengths and weaknesses. Additionally, it allows for improved managerial awareness of how to enhance reports’ quality. Finally, it helps international sustainability organizations (such as the GRI) showing them how to better shape their strategy and be successful.

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. First, the methodological approach is described; second, descriptive and thematic findings are presented; finally, a discussion, with future research directions, and conclusions, with contributions to theory and practice, and limitations, are provided.

2. Methodology
--------------

The systematic literature review methodology was chosen as it provides a rigorous and replicable approach ([Williams et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib144)). In particular, we adopted the three stages suggested by [Tranfield et al. (2003)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib132), i.e. planning, conducting the review, reporting and dissemination.

In the first and second stages (i.e., planning and conducting the review), following the identification of the need for a literature review on the topic, the research protocol was developed. The PRISMA diagram adopted for the literature search (e.g. [Graessler et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib48)) is shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#fig1).

![Image 12: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0959652624028774-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (310KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0959652624028774-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (310KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0959652624028774-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. PRISMA-chart of the literature selection procedure.

The search was conducted in April 2023 through the electronic database Elsevier's Scopus and included documents until December 2022. The keywords included were “GRI” and “Global Reporting Initiative”, which ensured that no relevant results were excluded. As for the boundaries of document selection, only the category “Article” was included, similar to previous studies (e.g. [Traxler et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib134)). Relevant documents cited in the analyzed papers were also included in the review using a snowballing approach, resulting in the addition of three papers. After the removal of duplicates, this initial phase yielded a total of 2992 documents.

The first round of exclusion was based on the year of publication, language and the subject area. Only English peer-reviewed articles and documents from 1998 were considered, given that GRI was established in 1997. The subjects included were “Economics, Econometrics and Finance”, “Business, Management and Accounting” and “Social Sciences”. These areas have also been selected by previous reviews on the related topic of sustainability reporting (e.g. [García-Sánchez, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib44); [Minutiello and Tettamanzi, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib89)). At the end of this phase, there were 931 papers. A second round of exclusion was applied to select only high-quality documents. In line with previous studies ([Turzo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib138); [Zahoor et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib149)), the rank provided by the Academic Journal list of the Chartered Association of Business Schools (CABS) was adopted as the selection criterion for journals’ inclusion. In particular, only journals ranked with Grades 2, 3, 4 or 4* were kept. After this procedure, the number of articles included was reduced to 352.

The final exclusion stage was based on the title, abstract, and full paper analysis. Papers were excluded if they did not focus on GRI, such as those analyzing the SDGs or IR. Additionally, topics deemed irrelevant, too specific (e.g., biodiversity disclosures), too broad (e.g., general sustainability reporting), or unrelated to business management were removed from the analysis. After this last round of exclusion criteria, a final sample of 90 peer-reviewed articles was considered.

Manual coding was then performed following a deductive-inductive approach to collect relevant information. A coding frame was defined a priori based on previous studies (e.g., [Orzes et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib101)) and then adjusted/integrated during the coding process. Specifically, it includes information on authorship, year of publication, type of study (empirical/theoretical), industry and geographical area, GRI version, theoretical grounding, methodology and main themes analyzed (see Appendix). Overall, the themes are structured based on an antecedent-process-outcome framework, as suggested by [Breslin and Gatrell (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib18). The “antecedents” cluster includes stakeholders’ pressures and factors influencing GRI adoption; the “process” cluster includes the topics of the evolution of GRI, the quality of the GRI reports and the comparison of GRI with other sustainability management tools; finally, the outcome cluster regards the impact of GRI on different aspects. As in previous literature reviews (e.g. [Toth-Peter et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib131)), discussions were held among the authors to ensure coding reliability and to reduce personal bias. Furthermore, comparisons with previous studies focused on the topic of sustainability reporting (e.g. [Ali et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib5); [Hahn and Kühnen, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib52)) and through the use of multiple sources of evidence have been performed, such as academic journals, websites, and webinars.

The last stage (i.e., reporting and dissemination) included the development of insights and critiques about GRI literature and the creation of linkages among the topics identified that allowed us to draw conclusions and future research directions.

3. Findings
-----------

In the following sections, the main findings are presented. Results are divided into descriptive and thematic findings. The descriptive findings provide insights into the distribution of papers over time, journal outlets, the sustainability dimensions, methodologies, and adopted theories. Thematic findings are a more in-depth description of the research topics based on the clusters that emerged from the review.

### 3.1. Descriptive findings

Academic interest in the GRI has grown over time, following the path of increasing attention to sustainability, Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) and non-financial Reporting ([Minutiello and Tettamanzi, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib89); [Pranugrahaning et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib113); [Turzo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib138)). [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#fig2) illustrates this trend, where a peak can be observed in 2017. This increase may be justified by the introduction of Directive (2014)/95/EU on sustainability reporting, which was effectively enacted in 2017 ([Breijer and Orij, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#bib17), p. 335).

In terms of outlet journals, the top three by number of articles are _Journal of Cleaner Production_, _Journal of Business Ethics_ and _Business Strategy and the Environment_, as shown in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959652624028774#tbl1). The Global Reporting Initiative (known as GRI) is an international independent standards organization that helps businesses, governments, and other organizations understand and communicate their impacts on issues such as climate change, human rights, and corruption. The "GRI" refers to the global network of thousands of participants worldwide who contribute to the creation of reporting standards, use them in disclosing their sustainability performance, demand their use by organizations as the basis for information disclosure, or are actively engaged in improving the standards. GRI was actively involved in the development of the European Sustainability Reporting Standards") (ESRS) which were submitted to the European Commission by the Sustainability Reporting Board (SRB) of the European Financial Reporting Advisory (EFRAG) as a step towards implementation of the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD).

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/dc/GRI_%28Global_Reporting_Initiative%29_official_logo%2C_2020.png/250px-GRI_%28Global_Reporting_Initiative%29_official_logo%2C_2020.png)

The **Global Reporting Initiative** (known as **GRI**) is an international independent standards organization that helps businesses, governments, and other organizations understand and communicate their impacts on issues such as [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), human rights, and corruption.

Since its first draft guidelines were published in March 1999,[[1]](#cite_note-Folkert-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) GRI's voluntary sustainability reporting framework has been adopted by multinational organizations, governments, [small and medium-sized enterprises](/wiki/Small_and_medium-sized_enterprises "Small and medium-sized enterprises") (SMEs), [NGOs](/wiki/NGOs "NGOs"), and industry groups. Over 10,000 companies from more than 100 countries use GRI.[[3]](#cite_note-sustain-3)
According to the 26 October 2022 [KPMG](/wiki/KPMG "KPMG") Survey of Sustainability Reporting, 78% of the world’s biggest 250 companies by revenue (the G250) and 68% of the top 100 businesses in 58 countries (5,800 companies known as the N100) have adopted the GRI Standards for reporting. GRI is used as a reporting standard by a majority of the companies surveyed in all regions.[[4]](#cite_note-KPMG-4)[[5]](#cite_note-summary2022-5)

**GRI** thus provides the world's most widely used [sustainability reporting](/wiki/Sustainability_reporting "Sustainability reporting") standards.[[6]](#cite_note-Adams-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Zsóka-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Whitaker-8)
Under increasing pressure from different stakeholder groups, such as governments, consumers and investors, to be more transparent about their environmental, economic, and social impacts, many companies publish a [sustainability report](/wiki/Sustainability_reporting "Sustainability reporting"), also known as a corporate social responsibility ([CSR](/wiki/Corporate_social_responsibility "Corporate social responsibility")) or environmental, social, and governance ([ESG](/wiki/Environmental,_social_and_corporate_governance "Environmental, social and corporate governance")) report.
GRI's framework for sustainability reporting helps companies identify, gather, and report this information in a clear and comparable manner.
Developed by the [Global Sustainability Standards Board](/w/index.php?title=Global_Sustainability_Standards_Board&action=edit&redlink=1 "Global Sustainability Standards Board (page does not exist)") (GSSB), the GRI Standards are the first global standards for sustainability reporting and are a free [public good](/wiki/Public_good_(economics) "Public good (economics)").[[9]](#cite_note-standards-9)

The GRI Standards have a modular structure, making them easier to update and adapt.
Three series of Standards support the reporting process.
The GRI Universal Standards apply to all organizations and cover core sustainability issues related to a company’s impact on the economy, society, and the environment.
The GRI Sector Standards apply to specific sectors, particularly those with the highest environmental impact, such as [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels").
The GRI Topic Standards list disclosures relevant to a particular topic area.[[10]](#cite_note-Quelier-10)[[3]](#cite_note-sustain-3)[[9]](#cite_note-standards-9)[[11]](#cite_note-introduction-11)
GRI Standards and reporting criteria are reviewed every three years by the Global Sustainability Standards Board (GSSB), an independent body created by GRI.[[3]](#cite_note-sustain-3)
The most recent of GRI's reporting frameworks are the revised Universal Standards, which were published in October 2021, and came into effect for reporting in January 2023.[[10]](#cite_note-Quelier-10)[[9]](#cite_note-standards-9)

## History

The Global Reporting Initiative was developed in 1997 by the United States–based non-profits [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(organization) "Ceres (organization)") (formerly the Coalition for Environmentally Responsible Economies) and consulting agency [Tellus Institute](/wiki/Tellus_Institute "Tellus Institute"). Key individuals were Ceres President [Bob Massie](/wiki/Bob_Massie_(politician) "Bob Massie (politician)") and [Allen L. White](/w/index.php?title=Allen_L._White&action=edit&redlink=1 "Allen L. White (page does not exist)") of Tellus. Other influential thinkers who were board members of Ceres included [Joan Bavaria](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Bavaria&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joan Bavaria (page does not exist)") of the [Social Investment Forum](/wiki/Social_Investment_Forum "Social Investment Forum") (SIF), [Alica Gravitz](/w/index.php?title=Alica_Gravitz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alica Gravitz (page does not exist)") of [Co-op America](/wiki/Co-op_America "Co-op America") and [Paul Freundlich](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Freundlich&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paul Freundlich (page does not exist)") of the US-based Fair Trade Foundation. The initiative soon gained support from the [United Nations Environment Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Environment_Programme "United Nations Environment Programme") (UNEP).[[12]](#cite_note-BrownWP-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Brown-13)[[1]](#cite_note-Folkert-1)

GRI released an "exposure draft" version of the Sustainability Reporting Guidelines in 1999,[[14]](#cite_note-14) and the first full version in June 2000. Work immediately began on a second version which was released at the [World Summit for Sustainable Development](/wiki/Earth_Summit_2002 "Earth Summit 2002") in [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg") in August 2002—where the organization and the guidelines were also referred to in the Plan of Implementation signed by all attending member states.[[12]](#cite_note-BrownWP-12)

As early as 2001, GRI expressed its intention to institutionalize the organization with a headquarters in Europe.[[1]](#cite_note-Folkert-1) In April 2002, GRI was inaugurated as an independent organization in a ceremony hosted at the UN headquarters in New York. Its mission was to provide “stewardship of the Guidelines through their continuous enhancement and dissemination (GRI 2000 Guidelines).”[[13]](#cite_note-Brown-13)
Engineering consultancy DHV (now [Royal HaskoningDHV](/wiki/Royal_HaskoningDHV "Royal HaskoningDHV")) expressed a strong interest in the initiative following the publication of the draft guidelines, translating them into Dutch and holding its first seminar on disclosing and reporting on 7 December 1999. Influential figures in the Dutch adoption of GRI include Nancy Kamp-Roelands, Johan Piet and Piet Sprengers. DHV approached then-CEO Allen White and set up meetings with the Dutch government. By April 2002, GRI had decided to settle in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")[[1]](#cite_note-Folkert-1) where it subsequently incorporated as a non-profit organization and a Collaborating Centre of the United Nations Environment Programme.[[12]](#cite_note-BrownWP-12) It had an initial staff of 12 people. Although the GRI is independent, it remains a collaborating center of [UNEP](/wiki/UNEP "UNEP") and works in cooperation with the [United Nations Global Compact](/wiki/United_Nations_Global_Compact "United Nations Global Compact").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

GRI has managed to mobilize extensive contributions of time, knowledge and funding from a wide variety of individuals and organizations.[[16]](#cite_note-Chrun-16)[[12]](#cite_note-BrownWP-12)[[17]](#cite_note-Levy-17) UN Secretary General [Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan") described it as having a "unique contribution to make in fostering transparency and accountability of corporate activities beyond financial matters".[[13]](#cite_note-Brown-13)[[18]](#cite_note-Massie-18)
A key factor in GRI’s success has been its global multi-stakeholder network, which grew from about 200 organizations and individuals in early 2000 to over 2000 members by early 2002. The network provided a platform for analysis and feedback on the Guidelines, enabling diverse stakeholders to actively engage in their creation and evolution.[[12]](#cite_note-BrownWP-12)
The initial organizational structure of the GRI was highly efficient and communicated mostly electronically. It consisted of a secretariat, a steering committee, and multiple decentralized working groups. Input from the working groups led to the expansion of GRI's scope from environmental reporting to three categories of sustainability indicators: social performance
indicators, economic performance indicators and environmental performance and impacts.[[12]](#cite_note-BrownWP-12)

The GRI system was created with the goals of standardizing practices for non-financial reporting, and empowering stakeholders at all levels with "access to standardized, comparable, and consistent environmental information akin to corporate financial reporting."[[12]](#cite_note-BrownWP-12) The process of aligning and standardizing practices has continued through multiple versions, with some debate over definitions of materiality to be used in sustainability reporting and their implicatioins.[[6]](#cite_note-Adams-6)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Jones-20) The GRI and the [Sustainability Accounting Standards Board](/wiki/Sustainability_Accounting_Standards_Board "Sustainability Accounting Standards Board") (SASB) illustrate two major approaches to materiality, with differences that may cause confusion in interpreting information about “material sustainability issues”.[[21]](#cite_note-Jørgensen-21)[[6]](#cite_note-Adams-6)

## Governance

The "GRI" refers to the global network of thousands of participants worldwide who contribute to the creation of reporting standards, use them in disclosing their sustainability performance, demand their use by organizations as the basis for information disclosure, or are actively engaged in improving the standards. Examples of good sustainability reporting practices include digitalization of supply-chain management, stakeholder relation mechanisms, and communication strategies that encourage conjoint two-way sense making and sense giving.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The governance structure for the permanent institution was approved on June 21, 2002. The institutional side of the GRI, supporting the network, is made up of the following governance bodies: board of directors, stakeholder council, technical advisory committee, organizational stakeholders, and a secretariat. Diverse geographic and sector constituencies are represented in these governance bodies.[[23]](#cite_note-Woods-23)

## Reporting guidelines

### Standards for guidelines

The GRI standards aim to enable third parties to assess environmental impact from the activities of the company and its supply chain.[[24]](#cite_note-Willis-24)
The most recent of GRI's reporting frameworks are the revised Universal Standards, which were published in October 2021, and came into effect for reporting in January 2023.[[10]](#cite_note-Quelier-10)[[9]](#cite_note-standards-9)
The GRI Universal Standards apply to all organizations and cover core sustainability issues related to a company’s impact on the economy, society, and the environment.
The GRI Sector Standards apply to specific sectors, particularly those with the highest environmental impact, such as Oil and Gas, Coal, and Agriculture, Aquaculture and Fishing.
The GRI Topic Standards list disclosures relevant to a particular topic area. Examples include Waste, Occupational Health and Safety, Biodiversity, Energy, Diversity and Equal Opportunity.[[10]](#cite_note-Quelier-10)[[3]](#cite_note-sustain-3)[[9]](#cite_note-standards-9)

### ESG metrics

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Stakeholder_%28en%29.svg/250px-Stakeholder_%28en%29.svg.png)

Sustainability reporting aims to standardize and quantify the environmental, social and governance ([ESG](/wiki/Environmental,_social,_and_corporate_governance "Environmental, social, and corporate governance")) costs and benefits, derived from the activities of the reporting companies. Examples of ESG reporting include quantified measures of [CO2 emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions"), working and payment conditions, and financial transparency.[[13]](#cite_note-Brown-13)[[25]](#cite_note-Tocchini-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Österblom-26)

The development of GRI standards was influenced by policies in the fields of international labor practices and environmental impact, which it, in turn has influenced.[[13]](#cite_note-Brown-13) ISO 14010, ISO 14011, ISO 14012 and ISO 26000 set out a standard for assessing the environmental impact,[[27]](#cite_note-27) while [OHSAS 18001](/wiki/OHSAS_18001 "OHSAS 18001") laid down a health and safety risk management system.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The [International Labour Organization](/wiki/International_Labour_Organization "International Labour Organization") (ILO)'s eight core conventions outline specific groups or population that require special attention: women, children, migrant workers and their families, persons belonging to national or ethnic, linguistic, and religious minorities, indigenous peoples, and persons with disabilities.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The reporting standards set by the GRI ESG assessment and reporting were developed based on principles set in [OECD guidelines](/wiki/OECD_Guidelines_for_Multinational_Enterprises "OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises") for Multinational corporations and [UN Guiding Principles](/wiki/United_Nations_Guiding_Principles_on_Business_and_Human_Rights "United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights").[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Accountability, adoption and registration

The Global Reporting Initiative is one example of a transformative shift in accountability systems to one based on a network of actors, observation of global public concerns, new problem framing and an ideological shift.[[32]](#cite_note-Newell-32)
Initially GRI worked with a number of Data Partners to collect and process information about GRI reporting and sustainability reporting in general and reporting trends in specific countries and regions. This information was added to GRI's Sustainability Disclosure Database.
In the United States, the United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland the official GRI data partner was the Governance & Accountability Institute.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-who-34)

In 2020 GRI decided to discontinue its publicly accessible sustainability disclosure database as of April 2021, due to the overhead of maintaining the collection. The publicly available database had over 63,000 reports spanning nearly 20 years from hundreds of companies.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

It is still possible to register GRI Standards-based reports and other published materials through the GRI Standards Report Registration System. Under Requirement 9 of GRI 1: Foundation 2021, notifying GRI of the use of the GRI standards is a mandatory step in reporting for associated organizations. Responsibility for the quality and verification of such reports is the responsibility of the reporting organization and its stakeholders. While GRI no longer provides examples of reports, the reports of many organizations are available from company websites.[[36]](#cite_note-FAQs-36)

Under the 2021 guidelines, which are required for reporting as of January 2023, organizations may report either "in accordance" with GRI (more stringent) or "in reference" to GRI. Both options involve notification.[[10]](#cite_note-Quelier-10)

A key issue in the adoption of GRI Standards is that compliance is voluntary. Outcomes depend on the quality and type of information reported. Further, firms may selectively disclose sustainability information.[[6]](#cite_note-Adams-6)[[37]](#cite_note-Gunningham-37) Research suggests that resources for sustainability reporting may be insufficient and staff undertrained in many companies. GRI attempts to address this by providing training.[[6]](#cite_note-Adams-6)
In order to circumvent "[greenwashing](/wiki/Greenwashing "Greenwashing")" or falsified reporting, a financial institution can conduct an independent audit of the investee or enter into a dialogue with the top management of a company in question.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
Independent assurance of sustainability reports may be demanded by stakeholders, and third-party assurance is standard practice for many large and mid-cap companies, though often expensive. Absence of assurance is associated with lower quality and credibility of sustainability reporting.[[6]](#cite_note-Adams-6)

## Related laws and standards

### European Commission Directives

In December 2014, the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), on behalf of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), adopted the [Non-Financial Reporting Directive](/wiki/Non-Financial_Reporting_Directive "Non-Financial Reporting Directive") (NFRD) obliging large multinational corporations to provide non-financial disclosure to the markets. The law applies to public companies with more than 500 employees.[[39]](#cite_note-EC2014-39) Companies that would provide such a reporting would be required to report on environmental, social and employee-related, human rights, anti-corruption and bribery matters. Additionally, these large corporations would be required to describe their business model, outcomes and risks of the policies on the above topics, and the diversity policy applied for management and supervisory bodies.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The reporting techniques were encouraged to rely on recognized frameworks such as GRI's Sustainability Reporting Guidelines, the United Nations Global Compact (UNGC), the [UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights](/wiki/United_Nations_Guiding_Principles_on_Business_and_Human_Rights "United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights"), [OECD Guidelines](/wiki/OECD_Guidelines_for_Multinational_Enterprises "OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises"), International Organization for Standardization (ISO) 26000 and the International Labour Organization (ILO) Tripartite Declarations.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Sustainability reporting requirements for companies were further expanded in the EU's [Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive](/wiki/Corporate_Sustainability_Reporting_Directive "Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive") (CSRD) which took effect on 5 January 2023.[[42]](#cite_note-42) GRI was actively involved in the development of the [European Sustainability Reporting Standards](/w/index.php?title=European_Sustainability_Reporting_Standards&action=edit&redlink=1 "European Sustainability Reporting Standards (page does not exist)") (ESRS) which were submitted to the European Commission by the Sustainability Reporting Board (SRB) of the European Financial Reporting Advisory (EFRAG) as a step towards implementation of the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). GRI worked for interoperability between GRI's global Standards, which focus on impact materiality, and the ESRS' focus on double materiality.[[43]](#cite_note-ESGN-43)[[44]](#cite_note-SustainCase-44)[[45]](#cite_note-IBM-45) Double materiality requires public reporting of both sustainability factors affecting the financial materiality of the company and its outward materiality (how the company affects society and the environment).[[43]](#cite_note-ESGN-43)[[46]](#cite_note-Baumüller-46)

### International Sustainability Standards

As of 24 March 2022, GRI and the [International Financial Reporting Standards Foundation](/wiki/International_Financial_Reporting_Standards_Foundation "International Financial Reporting Standards Foundation") (IFRS) announced that they would collaborate to align the [International Sustainability Standards Board](/wiki/International_Sustainability_Standards_Board "International Sustainability Standards Board") (“ISSB”)'s investor-focused Sustainability Disclosures Standards for the capital markets with the GRI's multi-stakeholder focused sustainability reporting standards.[[8]](#cite_note-Whitaker-8) As of 6 June 2023, the ISSB issued its inaugural standards (IFRS S1 and IFRS S2) for sustainability-related disclosures in capital markets.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

# Global Reporting Initiative: what it is and how to do it

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/63721a84a9a62db8eb794541/6373a85f966bd6b79a9458c4_clock-time.svg)

â[**Global Reporting Initiative or GRI**](https://www.apiday.com/glossaries-posts/gri-what-is-the-global-reporting-initiative) **is an international independent standards organisation that helps businesses, governments and other organisations to better communicate and understand their impacts on diverse environmental social and governance issues such as climate change, human rights, and corruption.**

**Co-founded by Ceres, Tellus Institute, and UN Environment Programme in 1997, GRI develops various standards that are widely used by organisations for annual reporting and disclosures.**

## How do the GRI Standards work?

### Methodology

**The GRI Standards are a modular system comprised of three interconnected series of standards:**

**Altogether, these three standards provide flexibility for differing needs.**

**The practical guide to sustainability seporting using GRI and SASB Standards notes that the GRI supports broad and comprehensive disclosures on organisational impacts, in contrast with the SASBâs focus on a subset of financially material issues.**

### Requirements

**There are nine requirements for reporting in accordance with the GRI Standards.**

**If all requirements are not met fully, the organisation issuing the sustainability information cannot claim that the information is in accordance with the GRI Standards.**

**Instead, they may indicate that the reported information was prepared with reference to the GRI Standards.**

**The GRI Universal Standards GRI 1 also provides guidance for the required procedures for âReporting with reference to the GRI Standards.â**

### Key concepts and principles

**The four key concepts for the GRI Standards are listed in the GRI 1: Foundation 2021 document:**

**The GRI Standards also identify eight reporting principles that guide the quality and presentation of the reported information. Specific guidance on application of said principles is included in the GRI 1, as well as some suggested best practices for reporting.**

### Alignment with other frameworks and requirements

**The GRI Standards are aligned with the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the ILO conventions, and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises.**

**Thus, organisations can use the GRI Standards for reporting out on progress towards the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). GRI developed guidance for alignment with SDGs after the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development.**

**Moreover, the GRI Standards are compatible with standards, frameworks, and initiatives issued by other organisations.**

[**This page**](https://www.globalreporting.org/how-to-use-the-gri-standards/global-alignment/) **links the 2016 version of the Universal Standards with related sustainability initiatives such as the SDGs, the SASB Standards, BLab Impact Assessment, the Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP), to name a few.**

### Tools and resources

**The GRI Standards provide diverse tools for efficient and comprehensive sustainability reporting by organisations.**

**Their website includes an FAQ for general questions, a GRI content index template, and an inventory of certified software partners by country, among other resources.**

**Moreover, as the GRI Standards are marketed as a free public good, the tools are available in the public domain free of cost and usually in English language.**

**GRI certification processes are also available to third party software or tool providers who want to be GRI-approved, along with a document on GRIâs pricing policy for certification.**

![ESG maturity tool lead magnet](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6373a0aa6003658a68c4fa48/6516a997b23002563d9a20df_Lead%20Magnet%20Banners%20for%20Blog%20Articles.png)

### Recognition

**The GRI Standards are widely recognised in the sustainability field, as well as specifically incorporated into other third-party sustainability assessments, such as Ecovadis.**

**Thus, information that is gathered and presented in accordance with the GRI Standards will likely garner high sustainability ratings.**

## Benefits of using the Standards for for-profit companies

**Potential benefits of using the GRI Standards are improved credibility of the organisation and satisfaction of stakeholders plus shareholders.**

**For instance, a 2017 joint study by the Government & Accountability Institute and Baruch College, City University of New York found that out of 572 total companies, the 481 companies that utilised the GRI Standards scored higher on information quality and degree of verification of ESG disclosure than the 91 counterparts who did not use the Standards.**

**Applying the GRI Standards to sustainability reporting also helps companies gain a deeper understanding of their material issues, as the Standards provide a blueprint for high-quality data collection and thorough reporting.**

**This understanding will aid companies in other endeavors, such as risk assessment and future projections.**

**Moreover, as ESG reporting becomes more commonplace in the private sector, many stakeholders (such as consumers, investors, and employees) expect some sort of formal sustainability reporting process as a matter of due diligence.**

**The Harvard Law School Forum on Corporate Governance reports that over 96% of 2021 Sustainability Reports included a letter from the CEO, signaling to stakeholders that ESG is a priority for senior leadership.**

**76% of 2021 Sustainability Reports were accompanied by a press release publicising the data, and a majority of companies also included a materiality matrix in their reports to provide stakeholders with more detailed insight into the companyâs sustainability strategy.**

**Given that the GRI Standards was the first globally accepted sustainability standard, it is a reliable starting point for companies that are looking to get started with sustainability reporting.**

## How to apply the GRI Standards

### Process overview

**The GRI reporting process is fairly open-ended as it provides guidance via its Standards, while giving the flexibility to organisations to determine the details of the process according to their needs.**

[**The graphic**](https://i.ibb.co/dm34VGn/tableaugri.png) **is from the GRI Standards introduction document, and lays out the typical steps for reporting under the GRI Standards.**

**The GRI does not require an audit phase, and there is no set timeline for the assessment. The Global Sustainability Standards Board (GSSB) does set out a new work program (involving projects to review existing Standards and develop new ones) every three years to keep the GRI Standards up to date.**

**Thus, it is recommended that companies using the GRI Standards review and publicly disclose relevant information periodically and with due diligence. This is usually being done annually by organisations in the form of sustainability reporting. Reports may contain a GRI content index.**

**The content index makes reported information traceable and increases the reportâs credibility and transparency.**

**GRI recommends that organisations report in accordance with the Standards, meaning that the organisation reports on all its material topics and related impacts and how it manages these topics.**

**However, if this is not feasible, the organisation can choose which content of the GRI Standards to apply and report with reference.**

### What changed with the 2021 revision of Universal Standards?

**The Universal Standards were revised following recommendations from the GRI Technical Committee on Human Rights Disclosure (a stakeholder group for labor-related disclosures).**

**The revisions were developed according to a set of mandatory requirements for standard development, and were overseen by the GSSB Due Process Oversight Committee and approved in July 2021.**

**The changes will officially come into effect on January 1, 2023, although GRI encourages earlier adoption when possible.**

## Frequently Asked Questions

Applying the GRI Standards to sustainability reporting helps companies gain a deeper understanding of their material issues, as the Standards provide a blueprint for high-quality data collection and thorough reporting. This understanding will aid companies in other endeavors, such as risk assessment and future projections.

The GRI Standards are a modular system comprised of three interconnected series of standards:
The GRI Universal Standards, which are applicable to all organisations on a broad level;
The GRI Sector Standards, which are applicable to specific sectors, and;
The GRI Topic Standards, which are each dedicated to a particular topic and listing disclosures for that topic.

The five steps process in the GRI reporting process are: understanding the key elements and the system of the GRI standards, applying the reporting principles through the process, identifying and assessing impacts while determining material topics, reporting information, publishing information and notifying GRI.

Pragastis Tabitha Bailey Design and layout Scribble Design © Stichting Global Reporting Initiative 2023 3 ●	GRI 2-22 Letter from Eelco van der Enden, CEO of GRI 2022 was a year of transformation for GRI on many fronts, and it was a good year. During 2022, our priority focus was set on standards development, global policy engagement and strengthening our brand and financial resources. We saw the publication of new Sector Standards, covering coal, agriculture, aquaculture, and fishing, as well as Topic Standards. In March, we signed a Memorandum of Understanding with the IFRS Foundation, committing both our organizations to coordinate work programs for sustainability-related standard setting activities. We also continued working jointly with EFRAG (former the European Financial Reporting Advisory Group) on the development of European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS). Next to that, our work in the field of engagement, capacity and community building was crucial – without it we would not be where we are today. As an example, participation in the GRI Academy continued to grow with over 800 individuals becoming GRI Certified Sustainability Professionals.
GRI is a transforming factor and frontrunner in the world of sustainability reporting, and in 2022 we celebrated GRI’s 25th anniversary. Taking time to reflect on this significant achievement, we see that our brand and message to the world are stronger than ever before. Today, the GRI Standards are ‘the global standards for sustainability reporting on impacts.’ This is reflected in KPMG’s 2022 Survey of Sustainability Reporting,1 which states that 78% of the largest 250 companies (G250) and 68% of the leading 5,800 companies (N100) in the world reporting on sustainability use GRI Standards. Next to that, GRI offers the only reporting standards used by a majority of surveyed companies in all regions (75% in the Americas, 68% in Asia-Pacific and Europe, 62% in ME and Africa). We understand the importance of local engagement and delivery and over the years, GRI has established representation on almost every continent. We will continue to leverage our global reach by strengthening our GRI Networks and growing our team in the different regions. This will further contribute to the overall impact of the GRI Standards, which were more popular than ever in 2022, with 974,000 downloads – a 45% increase compared to 2021. 1 https://home.kpmg/xx/en/home/insights/2022/09/survey-of-sustainability-reporting-2022.html Introduction 4 ●	GRI 2-3 ●	GRI 2-22 However, it is more than a numbers game: GRI’s position as the preferred supplier of sustainability standards for reporting on impacts has not gone unnoticed by funders, regulators and capital markets. As a result, in 2022 we were invited to key panels, boardroom discussions and meetings with regulators and government officials to expand the reach of GRI.
Our financial position and earned revenue are solid due to our grants and revenue generating capabilities. The revenue outlook is positive for all our lines of service – Training & Certification, Report Services, Licensing & Tools, Advisory and Community. For the GRI Networks, we plan to increase stakeholder engagements and drive our services. Looking ahead Looking to 2023, we will keep strongly emphasizing the message that there is no sustainability reporting without impact reporting, and GRI is the preferred supplier of standards for reporting on impact. GRI is now well positioned and can play its role as a key contributor to the ESRS standards, particularly due to our complementarity to the ISSB. GRI has become an organization where 40% of employees work remotely from around the world beyond the Netherlands; by the end of the year, this may be as high as 50%. Therefore, we need to have open lines of communication. With the prospect of growing our revenues, we have the means to invest in our people and organization to achieve our purpose. We will continue to professionalize the organization to achieve: ● Simple and effective operational processes for both business activities and fundraising; ● Strong and agile back office; ● Monitoring progress and performance measurement for progress tracking; ● Good corporate housekeeping (reporting and compliance); and ● A simple organizational structure.
Behind all of this progress is a strong global team of sustainability professionals, GRI’s most valuable asset, to whom I extend gratitude as well as congratulations. The GRI team around the world is dedicated to creating a sustainable future enabled by transparency and open dialogue about impacts. I want to express my gratitude again for welcoming me over the course of this year, and look forward to continuing with you on this great journey going forward.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The GRI framework enables organizations to disclose extensive environmental, social, and governance impacts, ensuring stakeholders receive a holistic view of sustainability practices.
GRI emphasizes inclusivity, requiring organizations to consider diverse stakeholder interests, enhancing transparency and fostering accountability in sustainability reporting.
GRI's standards provide structured guidelines that promote clear and consistent reporting, enhancing the reliability and comparability of sustainability information across organizations globally.
